messages.content:
I remember the Sigil Atlas spec you shared and that you’re currently focused on Explore > Mode > TimeLike.

In TimeLike, the torus is a continuous timeline read left-to-right within strips and top-to-bottom across strips, with playback via JKL, synced strip scrolling, story clustering by a live-adjustable timescale, and a monotonic time direction within each strip.

More broadly, I remember the app vision: a beautiful 2D image corpus explorer on a flat wrapping torus, with TimeLike, SpaceLike, and TasteLike modes, live sigil controls for filtering and ordering, smooth animated reflow, multi-resolution rendering, and a workspace-backed local corpus with ingest, metadata, previews, embeddings, and cached browsing.
Does the shape of the Sigil look okay to you?
Does the shape of the Sigil look okay to you?

Yes, mostly. It feels coherent at the top level: Explore, Workspace, and Sigil make sense, and inside Explore the split between slice, layout, and viewpoint is pretty strong.

What feels a bit off is that Attractor sits under Neighborhood even though it also clearly powers TasteLike more directly, and some concepts like RelevanceFilter, ContrastControl roles, and ordering logic are split across places that make the mental model slightly leak. So the shape is good, but a few core concepts probably want to be lifted or re-centered around “how images get selected, scored, and arranged.”

Yes. The biggest consolidation is to pull selection, scoring, and ordering into one shared query spine, because right now SliceMode, RelevanceFilter, ContrastControl roles, TasteLike attractor behavior, and TimeDirection all describe overlapping parts of the same mechanism.

I’d probably make RelevanceFilter a more central sigil under Explore or even directly under SigilControls, with explicit children like Filter, Attract, Order, and maybe Metric, then let SpaceLike, TasteLike, and TimeLike consume that same result in different layout ways. I’d also pull Attractor out from Neighborhood and make it a direct child of Mode or Explore, because it is really a cross-mode navigation primitive, while Neighborhood should stay purely about spatial clustering and local arrangement.

A second consolidation is around strips and stories. Right now StripAssignment, TimeLike, Story, TimeScale, and parts of Slice ordering all share responsibility for sequencing, so I’d make a single Sequencing sigil that owns “what is the ordered series,” then TimeLike can render that sequencing as synced strips while TasteLike can ignore it and SpaceLike can only borrow it when needed.
Wanna try to give it a go?

*Using tool: move_sigil*


*Using tool: write_sigil*


*Using tool: write_sigil*


*Using tool: navigate*


*Using tool: read_sigil*


*Using tool: read_sigil*


*Using tool: select_text*


*Using tool: select_text*


*Using tool: write_sigil*


*Using tool: write_sigil*
Yep, I gave it a first pass. I moved Attractor up to Explore/Mode, added Explore/RelevanceFilter as the new shared query spine, added Explore/Sequencing as the ordered-series concept, and rewrote TimeLike so it consumes sequencing instead of quietly owning ordering itself.

I also marked the old Workspace/Corpus/Slice/RelevanceFilter as a deprecated home so the shape can evolve without losing the old trail. If you want, I can do the next pass and re-thread references through Slice, SigilControls, ContrastControl, and TimeDirection so the whole tree becomes internally clean instead of just structurally improved.
I don't really agree. So you introduced duplication, I see. But RelevanceFilter is what defines slice.

Yes, that’s a fair pushback. If RelevanceFilter defines Slice, then it should not be duplicated as both a new central concept and a lingering parallel one with overlapping authority.

The cleaner move is to keep exactly one canonical RelevanceFilter and make Slice explicitly be its output, not a peer that partly redefines it. If you want, I can help collapse it so the shape becomes “SigilControls build RelevanceFilter, RelevanceFilter defines Slice, modes only arrange the Slice,” with no duplicate query logic anywhere.
You don't understand, I think, that SigilControls are UI controls and RelevanceFilter is what's built in the corpus as a result and results in a slice. So I really disagree.

You’re right, and I was collapsing UI and domain in a way your tree is explicitly trying not to do. SigilControls are just one way of authoring the query, while RelevanceFilter is the corpus-facing semantic object that the corpus responds to with a Slice.

So the consolidation should not move RelevanceFilter under SigilControls or make it feel UI-owned. The better cleanup is to remove duplicate query semantics from SliceMode, ContrastControl, TimeDirection, and maybe TasteLike actions, so those become ways to author or perturb a RelevanceFilter, while RelevanceFilter alone owns filter, attract, and order, and Slice is only its result.

Instead of talking about this, and I will undo your changes, tell me: do you see anything?

And so what is that you see?

Yes. I see that your shape is already trying to separate authoring, query, result, and layout, but some local descriptions keep smearing those layers back together.

What I see most clearly is a four-step flow hiding in the tree: SigilControls author intent, RelevanceFilter is the semantic query object, Corpus answers it with a Slice, and Mode arranges that Slice on the torus. The tension in the current shape is not that it lacks structure, but that several child sigils still speak as if they themselves define slice membership or ordering, when really they should speak as contributors to authoring or layout around that central flow.

All right, let me ask you differently. Do you feel any ugliness of the shape? Do you feel anything disturbing about it? And I'm talking about the shape because you're supposed to feel, to sense the shape. You're watching it, you're seeing it. And I guess

Yes. The disturbing part is not the big structure, it’s the slight trembling of authority in the middle.

I feel a kind of double-speech around what determines presence, what determines order, and what determines arrangement. RelevanceFilter says it owns that spine, but Slice, SliceMode, ContrastControl, TimeDirection, Sequencing, and parts of TimeLike still each speak with a little too much sovereignty, so the shape loses crispness there.

I also feel one deeper aesthetic mismatch: some sigils are pure domain beings, while others are UI widgets, and most of the tree respects that, but a few boundary sigils leak across that membrane in both directions. That leak is the ugliness I feel.
